title: DevOps

title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
headingPath: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads
text: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations for GitOps Workloads Summary Open with the practical thesis: a Kubernetes restore plan is only credible when it covers desired state, cluster API objects, persistent volume data, application level state, secrets, and control plane state separately. Argo CD uses Git repositories as the desired state source and continuously compares live state against that target, while Velero backs up Kubernetes objects and persistent volumes and etcd snapshot restore is a separate control plane recovery path [18][10][9]. Do not let the page imply that any single tool is enough to recover a production service. 18 10 Frame the operator's job as matching recovery mechanisms to failure modes and business objectives. RTO is the maximum acceptable outage duration and RPO is the maximum acceptable age of recoverable data, so backup schedules, storage location durability, restore automation, and drill cadence should be chosen to meet those targets rather than copied from a generic best practice checklist [21]. NIST CSF 2.0 treats recovery as restoration of affected assets and operations, which supports writing this page as an operational readiness guide, not merely a backup installation note [22]. 18 10 State the default architecture: GitOps for desired state, Velero for Kubernetes objects and volumes, application native procedures for databases, and etcd snapshots for the control plane [18][10][9]. 18 10 Make the reader choose explicit RPO/RTO examples such as "configuration restore within 30 minutes" or "database data loss below 15 minutes" before selecting backup frequency or tooling [21]. 18 10 Treat every restore as a production change with a rollback decision, target namespace or cluster, and validation criteria before touching live traffic [10][16]. 18 10 Decision Matrix Build the matrix around recovery surfaces. For bad manifests or accidental object deletion, start with GitOps reconciliation and Velero object restore; for persistent volume rollback, prefer CSI snapshots when the storage driver supports Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot APIs; for long term or cross provider durability, add CSI Snapshot Data Movement; for unsupported snapshot backends, consider Velero File System Backup with its beta, live filesystem, and privilege caveats; for database consistency, use application native dump, WAL, or quiesce flows alongside Velero hooks [8][11][12][13][15]. 8 11 Make the tradeoffs explicit instead of presenting a single recommendation. Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot is a point in time request that depends on CSI support, while File System Backup can cover many volume types but reads live file systems and is less point in time consistent than snapshot approaches [1][13]. For zone constrained storage, StorageClass volumeBindingMode matters because Immediate binding can provision before Pod scheduling constraints are known, whereas WaitForFirstConsumer delays binding until the workload exists [3]. 8 11 Use GitOps and kubectl apply/diff when the desired state in Git is correct and the failure is drift or accidental Kubernetes object deletion [8][18]. 8 11 Use Velero CSI snapshot backup for PVC backed data when the CSI driver and VolumeSnapshotClass are known to work in the target cluster [1][11]. 8 11 Use CSI Snapshot Data Movement when storage snapshots are not durable enough, must survive storage system failure, or need to move across providers [12]. 8 11 Use File System Backup only with a documented acceptance of beta status, live filesystem consistency, hostPath limitations, and node agent security implications [13]. 8 11 Use etcd snapshot restore only for control plane disaster recovery, and treat it as creation of a new logical cluster, not a live object level rollback [9]. 8 11 Reference Architecture Describe the architecture as six layers. Layer 1 is Git, where Kubernetes manifests, Helm values, Kustomize overlays, and Argo CD application definitions live. Layer 2 is Kubernetes API object backup, where Velero captures API resources into object storage. Layer 3 is PV data protection, using CSI snapshots, CSI snapshot data movement, or File System Backup depending on storage support. Layer 4 is application consistency, where backup hooks or database native dumps flush or freeze state before a snapshot. Layer 5 is secrets and encryption, where API resource encryption and RBAC are prerequisites for safely preserving sensitive objects. Layer 6 is control plane etcd snapshots for cluster state disasters [18][10][11][12][15][6][7][9]. 18 10 Show the data flow in prose: scheduled Velero backups create timestamped recovery points, write Kubernetes object archives to object storage, and snapshot or copy volume data when configured [10][17]. During restore, the backup storage location should be treated as the source of truth and can be set read only to avoid accidental creation or deletion while recovery is underway [10]. The target restore should begin in an isolated namespace or clean cluster unless the incident requires an explicitly approved in place update [16]. 18 10 Keep Git repositories independently protected; GitOps cannot restore a cluster if the desired state repository, secrets workflow, or deployment keys are unavailable [18]. 18 10 Keep object storage for Velero backups separate from the failed cluster and restrict mutation during restore windows [10]. 18 10 Document each StorageClass reclaimPolicy and volumeBindingMode, because deletion defaults and topology binding affect whether a restored workload can attach storage [2][3]. 18 10 Include an etcd snapshot path only for control plane rebuild scenarios, with revision bumping guidance for Kubernetes consumers [9]. 18 10 Restore Runbook Start the runbook with triage, not commands. Identify the failure type, decide whether the target is a new cluster, a new namespace, or an in place repair, freeze unrelated deploys, and preserve evidence such as backup names, namespace names, PVC names, StorageClass details, and the desired RPO/RTO. Velero restores are non destructive by default and skip existing resources unless an update policy is selected, so the safest default drill is a clean namespace or clean cluster restore with namespace mapping [16][10]. 16 10 Then provide command templates with warnings. Supported examples include velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME namespace mappings old ns:new ns , velero restore create <RESTORE NAME from backup <BACKUP NAME parallel files download <NUM wait for fs restore or CSI snapshot data movement cases, and kubectl diff f <directory followed by kubectl apply f <directory for declarative object reconstruction [16][8]. For etcd, show only a fenced, clearly labeled control plane recovery template such as etcdctl snapshot save snapshot.db and etcdutl snapshot restore snapshot.db bump revision <N mark compacted data dir <output dir ; note that this starts a new logical cluster and must not be treated as a live namespace rollback [9]. 16 10 Preflight: record backup name, target cluster, target namespace, affected PVCs, StorageClass, VolumeSnapshotClass, application version, and traffic cutover condition [16]. 16 10 Restore safely first: use namespace mapping into a non serving namespace where possible, then validate pods, PVC binding, service discovery, and application integrity before exposing traffic [16]. 16 10 For Argo CD state, use the documented argocd admin export and argocd admin import path, and include the namespace flag when Argo CD is not installed in the default namespace [19]. 16 10 For declarative manifests, use kubectl diff before kubectl apply , and avoid mixing apply with create or replace management methods [8]. 16 10 For etcd restore, require a separate control plane change ticket and operator pair review because snapshot restore creates a new logical cluster and changes member identities [9]. 16 10 Failure Scenarios Cover common failure scenarios with the first safe move, the restore method, and the validation test. For accidental manifest deletion or drift, use GitOps reconciliation or declarative apply, then check that live state matches the desired commit [18][8]. For namespace level deletion, use Velero resource filters or namespace mapping to restore into a controlled target and validate CRDs, RBAC, Services, and PVC binding before exposing clients [14][16]. For a corrupted PVC, choose CSI snapshot restore or data movement if supported; otherwise consider FSB or application native restore with the documented consistency limitations [1][11][12][13]. 18 8 Include less obvious failures that create false confidence. If a database accepted writes during a volume snapshot and no application hook or logical backup was used, the storage image may not meet the application's consistency expectations even if the volume attaches successfully [15][10]. If a cluster restore targets a newer or different Kubernetes API surface, Velero requires the backed up API group/version to exist in the target cluster, so restore tests must include CRDs and operators, not only Deployments and Services [10]. If the incident is control plane loss, etcd disaster recovery is a separate path that uses a snapshot database to form a new logical cluster [9]. 18 8 Bad deploy or config drift: revert Git commit or apply known good manifests, then allow Argo CD to sync or use kubectl apply with diff preview [18][8]. 18 8 Deleted namespace: restore with namespace mappings into a quarantine namespace first, then validate application and data before traffic cutover [16]. 18 8 PV corruption: use CSI snapshot or data mover when available; use FSB only after accepting beta status and live filesystem limitations [11][12][13]. 18 8 Secret exposure: rotate credentials after restore and verify encryption at rest and RBAC before treating restored Secrets as safe [6][7]. 18 8 Control plane failure: follow etcd snapshot restore, revision bump, and compaction guidance; do not attempt to solve it with namespace level Velero restore alone [9]. 18 8 Operational Checklist Write this as a checklist that an on call or platform owner can run monthly and before risky migrations. Confirm that every production namespace has a declared recovery owner, RPO, RTO, backup schedule, retention policy, restore target, and validation test. Check that Velero scheduled backups are producing timestamped recovery points, TTLs match retention policy, and filters are scoped to the intended namespaces and resource kinds [17][14][10]. 17 14 Add storage and workload checks that prevent surprises. Inventory StorageClasses for reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion settings, and snapshot support; ensure StatefulSet volume retention is understood; verify that no persistent application data depends on Docker writable layers or Kubernetes ephemeral assumptions; and confirm that backup storage access is separated from normal cluster credentials [3][4][20][13]. Kubernetes CronJobs can run operational jobs, but missed schedule behavior and controller cadence mean successful backup completion needs monitoring rather than blind trust in cron syntax [5]. 17 14 Daily or per shift: check latest backup completion, age, size trend, and error count; alert when newest backup age exceeds the declared RPO [21]. 17 14 Weekly: perform at least one namespace mapped restore of a representative workload and record elapsed restore time against RTO [16][21]. 17 14 Monthly: restore a PVC backed StatefulSet and verify application level data, not only Pod readiness [4][11][13]. 17 14 Quarterly: run a control plane tabletop covering etcd snapshot location, restore commands, revision bump decision, and certificate or kubeconfig dependencies [9]. 17 14 After every storage driver or CSI driver change: re test VolumeSnapshotClass behavior and Velero restore into a non serving namespace [1][11][3]. 17 14 Common Pitfalls Lead with false assumptions. A StatefulSet keeps sticky Pod identity and stable storage, and Kubernetes intentionally avoids deleting associated volumes when scaling down or deleting the StatefulSet, but that is a data safety behavior, not a backup strategy [4]. Docker's writable container layer is also not a persistence layer: Docker documents that it does not persist after container deletion and is a poor choice for write intensive data, so the page should tell readers to use volumes or Kubernetes PVs for data that matters [20]. 4 20 Then cover restore specific traps. Velero backups are not strictly atomic, so highly active object changes can fall outside a backup [10]. FSB can back up many volume types but is beta quality, less consistent than snapshot approaches, and may require privileged node agent behavior in some environments [13]. Secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd by default, which means backup archives containing Secrets require encryption, least privilege access, and rotation procedures after exposure [6][7]. 4 20 Do not assume "pod is Ready" means "data restored correctly"; require application specific integrity checks [10][15]. 4 20 Do not enable broad restore filters in production without a target namespace or clean cluster plan [14][16]. 4 20 Do not keep only storage system snapshots when the storage system itself is in the failure domain; use data movement or object storage where durability requirements demand it [12]. 4 20 Do not rely on CronJob alone for backup assurance; monitor missed runs and backup freshness against RPO [5][21]. 4 20 Do not restore etcd snapshots casually; official guidance treats restore as a new logical cluster and recommends revision bumping for Kubernetes contexts [9]. 4 20 Maintenance Notes Make maintenance concrete. Update the page whenever Kubernetes, Velero, Argo CD, CSI drivers, StorageClasses, VolumeSnapshotClasses, backup bucket policy, encryption configuration, or database engine versions change. Velero restore depends on backed up API group/version availability in the target cluster, so CRD and operator upgrade plans must include restore validation, not just forward migration testing [10]. StorageClass changes need backup review because reclaimPolicy, volumeBindingMode, expansion behavior, and mount options directly affect restore outcomes [3]. 10 3 Define a practical cadence: daily backup freshness checks, weekly representative restores, monthly stateful workload restores, quarterly etcd disaster recovery tabletop, and pre migration restore drills before Kubernetes or storage upgrades. Use the NIST and DR planning framing to keep the focus on readiness: recovery actions should be ready before an incident, and RTO/RPO results from drills should feed improvements to schedules, automation, and documentation [22][21]. 10 3 Maintain a compatibility table for Kubernetes version, Velero version, CSI driver, VolumeSnapshotClass, StorageClass, object storage, and encryption settings [10][11][1][3]. 10 3 Record the last successful restore date and elapsed restore time for each critical workload, not merely the last successful backup time [21]. 10 3 Review Velero backup TTL and schedule owner reference choices so GitOps tooling does not prune or fight day two Backup and Restore resources unexpectedly [17]. 10 3 Review Secrets handling after every key management or RBAC change, and rotate restored credentials after suspected exposure [6][7]. 10 3 Keep Argo CD export/import commands pinned to the running version and namespace used in the cluster [19]. 10 3 Sources 1. kubernetes.io 2. kubernetes.io 3. kubernetes.io 4. kubernetes.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. kubernetes.io 9. etcd.io 10. velero.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. velero.io 14. velero.io 15. velero.io 16. velero.io 17. velero.io 18. argo cd.readthedocs.io 19. argo cd.readthedocs.io 20. docs.docker.com 21. docs.cloud.google.com 22. nvlpubs.nist.gov

title: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations
text: Kubernetes Backup and Restore Operations Summary Kubernetes recovery is not one backup job; it is a layered system that has to cover control plane state, API objects, persistent volumes, storage provider snapshots, and application data semantics. PersistentVolumes are cluster resources with a lifecycle independent of any individual Pod, and etcd stores Kubernetes API objects, so recreating Pods or redeploying manifests does not automatically recover the state that a production workload depends on [2][7]. A useful recovery note should therefore separate what is recovered by etcd snapshots, what is recovered by Velero, what is recovered by a CSI driver, and what must be recovered by the application or database itself [8][9][20]. 1 2 The practical baseline is a documented RPO/RTO target for each service, a scheduled backup path, an isolated restore test path, and explicit ownership for the storage backend. NIST contingency planning guidance treats recovery strategy and operational planning as part of a system lifecycle, and the same mindset fits Kubernetes: a backup design is not complete until the team can restore it, prove the recovered application is coherent, and repeat the process after upgrades or storage changes [1]. For write heavy stateful services, volume snapshots can be a useful crash consistency layer, but PostgreSQL style point in time recovery still depends on archived WAL that reaches back to the base backup [20]. 1 2 Assumption: this page targets Kubernetes clusters where Velero, CSI snapshot support, or both may be available, and where database engines may still need their own PITR or dump strategy [10][11][12][20]. 1 2 Operator goal: make the recovery path boring by rehearsing namespace restores, PV/PVC binding, storage class mapping, and application health checks before an incident [10][19]. 1 2 Decision Matrix The core decision is not whether to use Velero or snapshots; it is which layer owns which recovery objective. Velero is a strong fit for Kubernetes API objects, namespace scoped resources, and persistent volume restore workflows, while etcd snapshots protect the cluster control plane and database native recovery protects transaction streams that Kubernetes does not understand [7][8][9][20]. StorageClass and CSI details must be included in the decision because a restored PVC is only useful if the destination cluster has compatible provisioning, topology, reclaim policy, and snapshot behavior [3][4][12]. 2 3 For RPO, use the workload write rate as the reality check. A nightly namespace backup may be fine for stateless services and low change configuration, but a busy database needs either application native log replay, a shorter snapshot cadence, or both. For RTO, include the slowest step in the path: downloading file system backup data, creating a new volume from a provider snapshot, replaying WAL or oplog data, waiting for StatefulSet readiness, and updating ingress or load balancer targets [6][10][11][20][21]. 2 3 Manifest only or GitOps redeploy: choose for stateless workloads and declarative configuration; do not count it as PV or database recovery [2][6]. 2 3 Velero resource backup: choose for namespaces, CRDs, RBAC, Secrets, ConfigMaps, Services, and workload objects; define filters deliberately because include and exclude flags change what is captured [9][13]. 2 3 CSI snapshot: choose for PVCs backed by a CSI driver with snapshot support and documented durability; verify VolumeSnapshotClass selection and driver name compatibility before cross cluster restores [4][12]. 2 3 Velero File System Backup: choose when snapshot support is missing or when data must be moved away from the storage platform; avoid presenting it as equivalent consistency to storage snapshots because it reads live mounted filesystems [11]. 2 3 etcd snapshot: choose for control plane disaster recovery; verify snapshot status and keep the restore procedure separate from namespace/PVC restore jobs [7][8]. 2 3 Database native PITR or dump: choose when the application needs transaction aware recovery, such as PostgreSQL WAL replay, MongoDB method specific tradeoffs, or Redis persistence mode recovery [20][21][22]. 2 3 Reference Architecture A reference architecture should use four recovery lanes. The control plane lane captures etcd snapshots and verifies them with etcdutl. The resource lane captures Kubernetes API objects with Velero, including CRDs and cluster scoped dependencies when needed. The volume lane captures PVC data through CSI snapshots, cloud block snapshots, or File System Backup. The application data lane captures transaction aware artifacts such as WAL archives, database backups, MongoDB backup streams, or Redis persistence files, depending on the workload [7][8][9][11][12][20][21][22]. 4 7 The storage design should be provider aware without becoming provider dependent. Kubernetes VolumeSnapshot objects standardize snapshot requests, but the actual durability and portability depend on the CSI driver and storage provider [4][12]. AWS EBS snapshots are point in time, incremental backups and AWS says customers are responsible for regularly creating or automating them, which is a good reminder that a CSI capable cluster still needs an explicit snapshot schedule and retention policy [23]. Backup object storage should be locked down and monitored; S3 Object Lock can provide WORM style protection, but Velero also documents cases where object store immutability can conflict with backup metadata updates or deletion behavior, so this needs a provider and version test rather than a checkbox [9][24]. 4 7 Control plane lane: scheduled etcd snapshots, encrypted snapshot storage, status verification, and a documented new logical cluster restore path [7][8]. 4 7 Resource lane: Velero backups with explicit namespace, resource, and cluster scope filters; resource policies for reusable skip/snapshot/fs backup decisions [9][13]. 4 7 Volume lane: prefer CSI snapshots where durability and restore compatibility are proven; use File System Backup when snapshot support is absent or storage independence is more important than snapshot level consistency [11][12]. 4 7 Application lane: database native PITR or backup mechanisms for workloads whose correctness depends on transaction logs or engine specific recovery [20][21][22]. 4 7 Restore Runbook A restore begins with containment and evidence, not with a blind command. Declare the incident scope, stop or fence writers when data corruption is suspected, identify the restore point, record the current cluster version and storage driver versions, and collect Velero debug material before changing the target environment. Velero supports a debug bundle that includes version information, server and plugin logs, Velero managed resources, and backup or restore logs when specified, so it is a useful artifact for the incident record [19]. 2 6 The safest restore target is an isolated namespace or recovery cluster first, then production after verification. A typical pre change backup can use velero backup create <backup name include namespaces <namespace wait , while a namespace rehearsal can use velero restore create <restore name from backup <backup name namespace mappings <old :<new wait and then velero restore logs <restore name for evidence [9][10][13]. For control plane loss, use an etcd specific path such as ETCDCTL API=3 etcdctl endpoints $ENDPOINT snapshot save snapshot.db , etcdutl write out=table snapshot status snapshot.db , and an etcdutl restore with revision bump and compacted marking when restoring Kubernetes from an older snapshot [7][8]. 2 6 Step 1: choose the restore point by matching business RPO, Velero backup timestamp, volume snapshot readiness, and database log availability [9][12][20]. 2 6 Step 2: restore API objects and PVCs into a non production namespace or recovery cluster; use namespace mappings and storage class mappings when the destination differs from the source [10]. 2 6 Step 3: verify PV/PVC binding, StatefulSet readiness, service endpoints, secrets, config, and application level invariants before allowing traffic [2][6][10]. 2 6 Step 4: replay application native data logs or restore database backups when the workload requires point in time correctness beyond the volume snapshot [20][21][22]. 2 6 Step 5: capture final evidence: restore logs, application checks, data consistency checks, RPO achieved, RTO achieved, and any manual changes made during recovery [19]. 2 6 Failure Scenarios Control plane loss and workload loss are different incidents. If all control plane nodes or etcd members are lost, Velero namespace backups do not replace the need for an etcd snapshot because the Kubernetes API state itself lives in etcd [7]. If only an application namespace is deleted, restoring the namespace and its dependent cluster scoped resources may be enough, but the restore still has to account for PV/PVC behavior, custom resources, StorageClasses, and any database logs that live outside the Kubernetes backup [2][3][9][10][20]. 2 3 Storage failure modes deserve their own drills. A CSI snapshot can fail because the snapshot controller or driver is missing, the destination cluster uses a different CSI driver name, or the provider snapshot is not durable enough for the threat model [4][12]. File System Backup can recover volumes that lack native snapshots, but it reads live mounted filesystems and therefore may be weaker for hot database consistency unless paired with quiescing hooks or database native backup [11][14][20]. 2 3 Deleted namespace: restore to a temporary namespace first, inspect resources, then decide whether to restore in place or promote traffic to the recovered namespace [10][13]. 2 3 Corrupted database: stop writes, choose a pre corruption base backup or volume snapshot, replay application logs only to the desired point, and keep the bad volume read only for investigation [20][21]. 2 3 PVC binds to the wrong class: use Velero storage class mapping or restore resource modifiers, and verify provisioner, topology, and reclaim policy before starting the application [3][10][16]. 2 3 LoadBalancer changes identity: expect restored Services to receive different cloud load balancer identifiers, and plan DNS or CNAME updates as part of cutover [19]. 2 3 Webhook or CRD dependency blocks restore: restore CRDs and controllers in the documented order and verify discoverability before restoring custom resources [10]. 2 3 Operational Checklist Daily operations should prove that backups are being created, uploaded, and retained where the runbook expects them. Velero schedules use cron expressions, and scheduled backups are not taken until the next scheduled time unless an operator explicitly triggers one from the schedule [9]. A simple operating habit is to review the latest scheduled backup, inspect warnings, confirm expected namespaces and resource filters, and check whether any storage snapshots or file system backup uploads are still in progress before declaring the backup window successful [9][12][13]. 3 4 Weekly or monthly operations should prove that restores work. A rehearsal should restore a representative namespace into a mapped namespace, verify resource order and PVC binding, exercise restore hooks where needed, replay application data where required, and record RPO/RTO evidence. Repository maintenance and cache sizing also need attention: Velero runs repository maintenance as Kubernetes jobs, and backup repositories have configurable cache and full maintenance settings that can affect performance and cleanup behavior [10][15][17][18]. 3 4 Every backup window: verify the newest backup exists, status is complete, logs have no unexplained warnings, and expected volume backup method was used [9][11][19]. 3 4 Every restore test: use namespace mapping, validate storage class behavior, run application probes, and document achieved RPO/RTO [10][13]. 3 4 Every storage change: repeat CSI snapshot and restore tests after CSI driver, StorageClass, VolumeSnapshotClass, topology, encryption key, or region changes [3][4][12][23]. 3 4 Every database change: confirm WAL, oplog, dump, or persistence settings still match the service RPO and that application owners can perform replay or validation [20][21][22]. 3 4 Every quarter: review object storage retention, object lock behavior, credentials, repository maintenance jobs, restore hooks, and debug bundle capture procedure [14][15][18][19][24]. 3 4 Common Pitfalls The most common mistake is treating a successful backup command as a successful recovery plan. A Velero backup can contain the expected Kubernetes objects while still being unusable for the business objective because the database logs are missing, the destination CSI driver is incompatible, a StorageClass maps to different performance or topology, or the restore has never been tested under production like admission policies [3][10][12][20]. Another quiet failure is excluding cluster scoped resources during a namespace focused backup and then discovering that CRDs, StorageClasses, or RBAC dependencies are missing during restore [10][13]. 3 4 Another pitfall is assuming all volume backup paths have the same consistency model. CSI snapshots are point in time requests to a provider driver; File System Backup reads a mounted live filesystem and is documented as less consistent than snapshot approaches [4][11]. Backup hooks can reduce risk for filesystem level snapshots by running commands before and after backup work, but hooks are not a substitute for database native recovery when the engine requires WAL, oplog, or append only replay to meet RPO [14][20][21][22]. 3 4 Do not use kubectl delete backup <name n <veleroNamespace when the goal is to remove backup data from object or block storage; Velero documents different deletion semantics for kubectl deletion versus velero backup delete [9]. 3 4 Do not enable schedule owner references casually in GitOps managed environments; Velero documents side effects where schedule deletion and backup CR synchronization can conflict [9]. 3 4 Do not assume immutable object storage is automatically compatible with Velero; test the exact provider mode because Velero updates backup metadata during normal operation [9][24]. 3 4 Do not assume restored LoadBalancer Services keep the same cloud load balancer identity; plan DNS and external dependency updates [19]. 3 4 Do not rotate or relocate backup repository settings without testing old backup restores; repository configuration, cache, and maintenance behavior are part of the recovery path [17][18]. 3 4 Maintenance Notes Backup maintenance should be tied to the same change calendar as cluster upgrades, CSI driver upgrades, database major upgrades, and storage class changes. When the storage class, CSI driver name, snapshot class, or database engine changes, the previous restore evidence expires for that workload because the binding, snapshot, or replay path may have changed [3][4][10][12][20]. A mature runbook records the cluster version, Velero version, plugin set, CSI driver, StorageClass, VolumeSnapshotClass, and database recovery method alongside every successful restore rehearsal. 3 4 Repository maintenance is operational work, not background magic. Velero decouples repository maintenance into Kubernetes jobs, provides resource and affinity configuration, and exposes recent maintenance history; that makes maintenance schedulable and observable, but it also means resource starved clusters can starve backup maintenance if nobody owns it [18]. For large File System Backup repositories, tune cache and maintenance intervals carefully, because shorter full maintenance intervals can remove unused data sooner but may weaken data safety if applied incorrectly [17]. 3 4 Monthly: restore at least one representative namespace and one representative PVC path, then record RPO/RTO, commands used, backup IDs, snapshot IDs if available, and validation results [9][10][12]. 3 4 After upgrades: retest Velero backup, Velero restore, CSI snapshot creation, CSI restore, File System Backup, and application native database recovery [10][11][12][20]. 3 4 After credential or retention changes: verify that Velero can still list, read, restore, and delete according to policy, and that object lock settings do not block required metadata updates [9][19][24]. 3 4 For auditability: keep debug bundles, restore logs, application validation output, and a short incident style note for every successful and failed rehearsal [19]. 3 4 Sources 1. csrc.nist.gov 2. kubernetes.io 3. kubernetes.io 4. kubernetes.io 5. kubernetes.io 6. kubernetes.io 7. kubernetes.io 8. etcd.io 9. velero.io 10. velero.io 11. velero.io 12. velero.io 13. velero.io 14. velero.io 15. velero.io 16. velero.io 17. velero.io 18. velero.io 19. velero.io 20. www.postgresql.org 21. www.mongodb.com 22. redis.io 23. docs.aws.amazon.com 24. docs.aws.amazon.com
